Here we have focused on multi-filter speckle imaging on 4-m class telescopes of a sample of 57 systems, out of the 97 systems presented by V. B. Kos-tov et al. (2022). Of the 57 systems investigated here, we are able to resolve 34 (nearly 60%) into two distinct sources. Using C. A. L. Bailer-Jones et al. (2021) ge-ometric distances, we have calculated estimated linear separations between the resolved companions, and have placed upper limits on the linear size for the unresolved systems. Among the former are several quadruple sys-tems apparently with a projected linear separation the size of the Solar system - TIC 97356407 (32 au), TIC 146810480 (35 au), TIC 328181241 (38 au), and TIC 399492452 (30 au)

The SN and Cepheids are rungs on the cosmic distance ladder, but not at the bottom, and so still need parallax measurements. So far, these annual parallax measurements are readily measurable out to ∼10 kpc (Brown et al. 2018; Lindegren et al. 2018; Bailer-Jones et al. 2018). The mean parallax value is 0.20 ± 0.08 mas as calculated in section. The average parallax angle is adjusted for the Gaia DR3 parallax zero-point offset of -0.021 mas as proposed by Groenewegen (2021). As per Bailer-Jones (2015), simply inverting Gaia parallax is in-sufficient for distance estimation. They suggest a probabilistic approach to assessing distance and uncertainties from Gaia parallax data. As per Bailer-Jones (2015), simply inverting Gaia parallax is in-sufficient for distance estimation. They suggest a probabilistic approach to assessing distance and uncertainties from Gaia parallax data. This method uses parallax as well as its error and a prior, which depends on the location of the cluster in the galaxy. Following the methodology proposed by Bailer-Jones et al. (2018), we conducted a probabilistic analysis to ascertain the distance of the Berkeley 76 cluster. Employing the Github code1 developed by Bailer-Jones, we computed the distance to be 4.27 ± 0.91 kpc. This distance value closely aligns with the findings of Cantat-Gaudin et al. (2018)

Indeed, for stars fainter than G=18, the error bar on the parallax becomes larger than 0.2, which, given the distance of NGC 752, implies an error larger than 10%. This, in turn, implies that we can no more do a simple inversion to determine the distance of the stars. One can, instead, use the photogeometric distances determined by Bailer-Jones et al. (2021), which uses the parallax, colour and apparent magnitude of a star with a direction-dependent prior on distance, while exploiting the fact that stars of a given colour have a restricted range of probable absolute magnitudes (plus extinction). This is what we are using in the remaining of this paper. Gaia only provides radial velocities for objects brighter than around magnitude 13
